name: Steve Lacy
abstract: Steve Lacy (born Steven Norman Lackritz; July 23, 1934 – June 4, 2004) was an American jazz saxophonist and composer recognized as one of the important players of soprano saxophone. Coming to prominence in the 1950s as a progressive dixieland musician, Lacy went on to a long and prolific career.

concerts.label: Carnegie Hall Jazz Band: Music of Thelonious Monk: A New Perspective
concerts.venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage

concerts.label: Danilo Pérez Trio/ Danilo Pérez-Steve Lacy Lacy Duets

about_quotes.speaker: Roswell Rudd
about_quotes.evidence: Because I had a friend like Steve Lacy... While at Yale, Roswell performed Dixieland music with Eli’s Chosen Six and shortly after started an association with saxophonist Steve Lacy.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roswell Rudd interviewed by Monk Rowe, Ellenville, New York, June 23, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Levin
about_quotes.evidence: There was one European tour we did, the band was a little bigger than usual but the sax section was Dave Sanborn, Arthur Blythe, Steve Lacy and Howard Johnson and George Adams.
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Levin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Danbury, Connecticut, November 20, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Heath
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Steve Lacy only in response to the interviewer's prompt, without providing any personal anecdote, collaboration, or direct interaction history.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Heath — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bley Carla
about_quotes.evidence: I had the band and I would hire people to play with me... Steve Lacy playing Monk tunes, that’s where I learned all the Monk tunes
about_quotes.doc_title: Bley Carla — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Howard Mandel
about_quotes.evidence: up to Adrian Rollini up to Steve Lacy, Tim Berne, Dave Douglas, Myra Melford who I was hanging out with last night
about_quotes.doc_title: Howard Mandel interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Hentoff Nat
about_quotes.evidence: There were players – Steve Lacy, for example, started out playing traditional jazz.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hentoff Nat — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
